artifacts.content:
From: michael.anderson@gmail.com
To: emily.anderson@gmail.com
Subject: Trying to keep all the plates spinning

Em,

Kids are finally down and Max is snoring by the back door, so I’m grabbing 10 minutes to brain-dump before I crash. I keep thinking about how far things have come with my back and how sideways everything else feels sometimes.

Work first: ever since HR put me on that formal PIP on May 6, 2024 after we missed those two sprints and I snapped at Eric in the code review, I feel like I’ve been living at NorthRiver. I’m still doing 55–60 hour weeks at the Minneapolis office and half the time I’m in bed scrolling Slack until close to midnight because I’m terrified of missing something. My energy tanks by mid‑afternoon no matter how many stretch breaks I take.

It’s wild to remember that before all this, back in March 2024, my “big” goal was just to get my lower back pain under control enough to sit through an 8–9 hour day without sneaking those 10 mg hydrocodone Dr. Hart at Woodwinds gave me after the FMLA mess in November 2023, when Dana in HR pulled me in about my productivity. In a weird way, that part is actually better now – pain’s more like a 3–4 most days instead of a 7–8.

The stress side is another story. I’m still waking up around 3–4 a.m. a couple nights a week with my heart pounding, doing mental math about the mortgage and daycare. I know shutting the laptop by 9:30 helps, but between the PIP and trying to be present with you and the kids, something always gives.

Anyway, thanks for putting up with my zombie mode lately. Let’s protect that Duluth weekend if we can – I need something on the calendar that isn’t NorthRiver or a doctor.

– M

metadata.subject: Trying to keep all the plates spinning

artifacts.content:
From: michael.anderson@gmail.com
To: karen.lewis@gmail.com
Subject: Dad, Jacob, and my spine all acting up at once

Hey Karen,

Catching up between stand‑ups while my back timer is yelling at me to stand. I’ve been thinking about you a lot since Dad’s last visit and wanted to write more than a text thread.

Remember when I finally caved and started chiro in early May 2024? One of the big reasons was Jacob’s 90‑minute OT sessions at Gillette Children’s in St. Paul after his ASD eval with Dr. Nguyen that February. I promised I’d sit through every session, but my lower back and neck were at a constant 7–8/10 and I kept bailing to stand in the hallway. It felt awful breaking that promise to him.

At the same time, Dad was melting down in Arizona. After his minor stroke in April 2024 and the rehab at Sun City, I was trying to figure out how to get my back good enough to do that 1,600‑mile round trip to Scottsdale and help move him out of the condo without having to stop every 45 minutes to stretch or double‑up on the gabapentin the Regions neuro gave me. I even emailed Mom about it, remember? “Goal for chiro: survive the drive without living on gabapentin.”

Fast‑forward to now and the pain is way better – I can sit on the floor and actually help Jacob with his scoliosis PT routine from Gillette (the 20‑minute one Dr. Nguyen gave us) without spiking to a 7/10. But the emotional stuff around Dad’s health and Jacob’s diagnoses still knocks me sideways. I keep flashing back to when we helped admit Uncle Mark to Hazelden in March 2023 and wondering how much of this runs in the family.

Anyway, how are *you* doing with all of Dad’s Parkinson’s stuff and your own depression work with Dr. Patel at Regions? We should schedule a sibling Zoom that isn’t just crisis logistics.

Love you,
Mike

metadata.subject: Dad, Jacob, and my spine all acting up at once

artifacts.content:
Title: Health / stress brain dump – connect the dots

Lately I’ve been trying to make sense of how all these different health things stack on each other.

Back/neck: When I first started treatment in March 2024, Goal #1 was simple on paper: get pain down from 7–8/10 so I could sit through a full 8–9 hour day at NorthRiver without reaching for the 10 mg hydrocodone Dr. Hart at Woodwinds gave me after the November 2023 FMLA leave for pain + insomnia (and the awkward HR chat with Dana about “productivity”). Goal #2 was posture/flexibility so I could actually sit through Jacob’s 90‑minute OT at Gillette after his February 2024 ASD eval with Dr. Nguyen instead of pacing the hallway.

Work stress: Since HR dropped the formal PIP on May 6, 2024 for the missed sprints + blowing up at Eric, I’ve been back to 55–60 hour weeks and Slack doom‑scrolling in bed until midnight. Energy is weird: back pain wakes me less, but I’m still up 3–4 a.m. replaying Elena’s performance speeches and the fainting spell at the Minneapolis office last August that sent me to Allina Occ Med.

Family: Dad’s early Parkinson’s dx at Banner in Phoenix (Feb 2024) + his heart procedure at Mayo + Uncle Mark’s Hazelden stay in March 2023 + Karen’s depression with Dr. Patel at Regions = constant background hum of “you’re next.” Add Jacob’s ADHD eval at Children’s in November 2024 and the CPS visit on June 18, 2024 after he told the Bailey counselor I “yell a lot when I’m tired,” and no wonder my jaw lives clenched.

Action items: keep the nightly stretches, protect 9:30 p.m. laptop cutoff, schedule with Dr. Kim at Riverside again, don’t skip walks with Max even on PIP days.

metadata.title: Health / stress brain dump – connect the dots

artifacts.content:
From: michael.anderson@gmail.com
To: mom.linda.peterson@gmail.com
Subject: Quick update on my health (and the kids)

Hi Mom,

Figured I’d send a real update instead of the 30‑second “everyone’s fine” version I give you between Jacob’s Minecraft monologues.

Physically, I’m in a better place than last year. Since starting weekly telehealth CBT with Dr. Kim at Fairview Behavioral Health after that panic episode on February 6, 2024 (the one that landed me in the Regions ER with my heart over 140 and BP 162/98), my sleep is more like 6.5–7 hours straight most nights. On the back front, pain is usually around 3–4/10 instead of 7–8, and I can actually bend to pick up Sophie without that stabbing catch.

The GI stuff is calmer too. After Dr. Benson at M Health Fairview Digestive Clinic diagnosed IBS last March and put me on low‑FODMAP, plus the probiotic, the evening bloating is down maybe 70%. I still flare when work gets nuts or Emily and I are arguing about Jacob’s behavior at Valley Creek, but it’s not every night.

Stress is still high, though. HR at NorthRiver put me on a formal Performance Improvement Plan back on May 6, 2024 after we missed two sprints and I lost my temper in a code review. Since then it’s been 55–60 hour weeks and a lot of late‑night Slack checking. Add in Dad’s early‑stage Parkinson’s diagnosis at Banner in Phoenix in February 2024 and helping him sort that extra $600/month for meds, and my brain rarely shuts off.

The kids are hanging in there. Jacob’s OT at Gillette after his ASD eval with Dr. Nguyen is helping, and the 20‑minute scoliosis exercises we do on the living room floor four nights a week are getting easier on both our backs. Sophie is her usual tornado self.

We’ll try to come by Maplewood Sunday after her nap if my back and the weather cooperate.

Love,
Mike

metadata.subject: Quick update on my health (and the kids)

artifacts.content:
Title: DUI court / lawyer / class – DO NOT OVERBOOK

All-day notes for myself so I stop double‑booking work stuff on top of this mess.

Dec 12, 2024 – Ramsey County Courthouse, St. Paul
• Morning: initial DUI hearing for the stop on I‑94 near Woodbury after the team happy hour at The Liffey.
• Block off whole day on NorthRiver calendar (OOO – legal appointment). I’ll be a wreck; no sprint planning, no code reviews.

Dec 17, 2024 and ongoing – Mark Jensen’s office, 7th Street, St. Paul
• Every Tue/Thu 4:00–5:00 p.m. – prep meetings with Mark about the case, license, and the mandatory alcohol education weekend.
• Remember to leave downtown Minneapolis office by 3:15 to make it on time.

Jan 18–19, 2025 – Ramsey County Courthouse
• Court‑ordered weekend alcohol education program.
• Arrange rides: Emily may need to shuttle kids without my help if license gets restricted.

Context reminders:
– Liver enzymes were already elevated back in November 2023 with Dr. Desai at Woodwinds (early fatty liver warning after those 3–4 IPAs most nights). I’d actually cut back to 3–4 drinks a week and dropped 9 lbs, but clearly not enough to avoid this.
– Stress is already high with the Performance Improvement Plan at NorthRiver (since 5/6/24) and the CPS home visit last June 18 after Jacob told the Bailey Elementary counselor I “yell a lot when I’m tired.” Need to build in recovery time – walks with Max, stretching, early nights – or my back and neck will explode.

No driving after happy hours. Non‑negotiable.

metadata.title: DUI court / lawyer / class – DO NOT OVERBOOK

artifacts.content:
From: michael.anderson@gmail.com
To: pastor.greg@woodburycommunitychurch.org
Subject: Following up after July 7

Hi Pastor Greg,

Thanks again for meeting with Emily and me back on July 7, 2024. I’ve been meaning to follow up now that a little time has passed.

The night in June when I ended up sleeping on the couch after that huge argument about my late hours and snapping at the kids really shook me. For a couple of weeks afterward I was lying awake in the living room until 2 a.m., replaying the fight and wondering if I was turning into my dad. My afternoon energy at NorthRiver was shot – I’d hit 3 p.m. and feel like a zombie even though my back pain itself was better.

Some of that is tied to the bigger picture: the NorthRiver layoff scare last spring, the formal PIP HR put me on May 6, 2024, and the CPS home visit on June 18 after Jacob told the Bailey Elementary counselor I “yell a lot when I’m overtired.” Even though Ms. Rodriguez closed the case the same day, just hearing “Child Protective Services” connected to our house on Brookstone Lane was enough to keep me up at 3–4 a.m. with my heart racing.

On top of that, I’ve been uneasy about how often I reach for a double whiskey after the kids are down around 8:30 p.m. That New Year’s Eve 2024 conversation with you and Emily about my drinking creeping up has been on my mind, especially after the DUI on I‑94 in December and the weekend alcohol class I have to do at Ramsey County next month.

The good news is we’ve actually stuck to the “off laptop by 9:30 p.m.” boundary you suggested and scheduled two kid‑free evenings a month like Sarah at Riverbend Family Therapy recommended. My tension headaches and jaw clenching are less constant, and I’m a little more patient with Jacob and Sophie at bedtime.

Appreciate you listening,
Michael

Title: Money / work / health – late‑night worries

Sitting at the kitchen table on Brookstone Lane again at 10:15 p.m., spreadsheets open, trying not to panic.

End of July 2025 was the low point financially: checked our MidWest Community Credit Union joint account and saw we were down to $1,240 right before the mortgage auto‑draft on the 28th. Between the ~$18,000 on the Chase Sapphire + Target REDcard (medical, daycare, random emergencies) and pulling $2,400 from the emergency fund in late January 2025 to catch up after I forgot to reset the Wells Fargo auto‑pay post‑refi, it feels like we’re always one surprise away from disaster.

Layer on work: still under the shadow of the May 6, 2024 Performance Improvement Plan at NorthRiver, plus the earlier near‑miss with UnitedHealthcare almost denying my short‑term disability claim in March 2023 as “non‑work‑related lumbar disc degeneration.” I started chiro in July 2024 partly so I could sit through sprint planning and code reviews without my back locking up and triggering another round of ergonomic reviews and HR meetings.

Energy/stress: the panic attack in Paul Jenkins’ office on March 12, 2024 about the missed Q1 delivery led to weekly 7:30 p.m. telehealth with Dr. Hart at Fairview and three weeks of FMLA in April. It helped, but I’m back to waking up at 2–3 a.m. some nights, running numbers in my head and worrying about Jacob’s school issues, Karen’s depression with Dr. Patel at Regions, Dad’s Parkinson’s meds at Banner, and whether Emily is secretly resentful about carrying more of the load.

Reminder to self: keep walking Max, keep the stretches, keep the 9:30 p.m. laptop cutoff. Call Dr. Kim at Riverside about restarting the 12‑week CBT so this doesn’t spiral.

From: Michael Anderson <m.anderson@northriveranalytics.com>
To: Emily Anderson <emily.anderson@email.com>
Date: 2025-02-11
Subject: Why I really need to stay on top of treatment

Em,

I wanted to write this out because everything’s starting to blur together.

When I first started care back in late August 2024, my main goal was honestly pretty specific: I needed my back and neck calm enough to sit through that 3‑hour co‑parenting mediation you scheduled at the Washington County Courthouse in Stillwater on September 10, 2024 about our trial separation and the custody plan for Jacob and Sophie. I kept picturing myself having to duck out every 20–30 minutes to stretch in the hallway and looking like I didn’t care about the co‑parenting mediation or custody details. That was kind of the wake‑up call.

Same pattern at work. After the failed Q1 2024 product launch and those 12‑hour days at the NorthRiver Analytics office in downtown St. Paul, the sciatic pain got so bad I kept leaving sprint planning and code review meetings. That’s what led to the written performance improvement warning Elena Martinez put in on April 22, 2024. Goal #2 with treatment was to be able to sit through full‑day sprint planning without risking another write‑up.

Now in early February 2025, I’m on a formal performance improvement plan with Elena Ruiz after the missed release deadlines. I’m staring down 55–60 hour weeks at the downtown Minneapolis office and waking up around 3–4 a.m. with chest tightness, worrying about losing the job. Keeping my back and neck under control is the only way I can survive this PIP and still be present for you and the kids.

Just wanted you to know there’s a bigger plan behind all these appointments.

– Michael

metadata.subject: Why I really need to stay on top of treatment

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Anderson <m.anderson.personal@email.com>
To: Linda Peterson <linda.peterson@email.com>
Date: 2025-03-05
Subject: Health / work / kid update (it’s a lot)

Mom,

Figured I’d give you a fuller update instead of piecemeal texts.

When I started treatment in March 2024 my second big goal was being able to fly down monthly to Dad’s condo in Sun City, Arizona without my lower back seizing up like it did March 15–17, 2024. That was the trip where I spent almost half a day lying on his living room floor instead of helping him pack boxes after his minor stroke rehab. I don’t want a repeat of that.

Then in June 2024, after the rough Child Protective Services home visit at our place on Brookstone Lane on May 3, Emily and I enrolled in that 8‑week, 90‑minute Thursday night parenting class at the Woodbury Community Center. My second goal there was just to sit through the full 90 minutes without my back tightening so much I had to pace around the room.

More recently, starting March 2025, my third main goal has been to get my neck and upper back pain down enough to stop relying on the leftover oxycodone Dr. Patel at Woodwinds Hospital prescribed after my minor car accident on I‑94 in October 2023. This past winter I was taking 1–2 pills most evenings just to get through bedtime with Jacob and Sophie, and I’ve been worried about depending on them.

On the emotional side, since early September 2025 I’ve been a little steadier, even after the panic attack in the NorthRiver Analytics parking garage in Woodbury on October 3, 2025 when Elena Ruiz told me HR was opening a “performance improvement review” because I’d missed two sprint deadlines. I’ve been seeing a therapist, Dr. Karen Holt, at Riverbend Behavioral Health in St. Paul every other Thursday at 6:00 p.m. for CBT, and between that and the reduced pain I don’t feel quite as hopeless as over the summer.

Love,
Michael

metadata.subject: Health / work / kid update (it’s a lot)

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Anderson <m.anderson.personal@email.com>
To: Karen Lewis <karen.lewis@email.com>
Date: 2025-11-02
Subject: Catch‑up: Dad, Jacob, your case, and my back

Hey Karen,

Long overdue update.

Physically, things really started to turn around after I began care in October 2024. I can now sit through our full 90‑minute sprint planning meetings at the NorthRiver Analytics office in downtown St. Paul with you without that burning pain shooting down my right leg. Before that, I was constantly shifting and even had to leave a meeting early once, which led to the informal performance discussion with HR on October 18 about whether my back problems were affecting my ability to lead the team.

Since mid‑November 2024 I’ve also been able to handle longer walks pushing Sophie’s stroller around the block near Brookstone Lane. When Dad visited from Arizona after his minor heart procedure at Mayo Clinic, we did about 40 minutes on November 23, and my back stayed around a 3/10 instead of flaring.

January 2025 was another test: I spent roughly three hours on a Saturday hauling six boxes of 2017–2020 tax documents (25–30 lbs each) up from the basement at the Woodbury house after we got that IRS notice about a discrepancy on our 2019 return so I could send everything to Larson & Pike CPAs. My back only hit about a 4/10 instead of locking up.

Emotionally, it’s still been rough. In April 2025, after Jacob got into that shoving incident at Valley Creek Elementary in Woodbury, the principal, Ms. Larson, called us in for a behavior meeting. I was such a wreck I sat in the school parking lot and cried beforehand and ended up emailing the school counselor, Mr. Patel, at 11:30 p.m. one night asking if he thought Jacob might have ADHD.

Your own custody mess has been brutal to watch. Since I started adjustments in late August 2025 I’m a bit more hopeful overall, but when I had to testify via Zoom from our home office on Brookstone Lane on September 19, 2025 in your Ramsey County family court hearing about Daniel showing up drunk at Sophie’s 4th birthday party back in May, I was wiped out for days afterward—tight chest, racing thoughts, snapping at everyone.

On top of that, around January 2025 one of my big goals was to improve sleep and morning stiffness so I’d stop missing the 8:30 a.m. stand‑up at NorthRiver. After Jacob’s ADHD evaluation at Children’s Hospital of Minnesota in November 2024 and all the late‑night behavior issues, I ended up with two informal warnings from my manager, Elena Rodriguez, about being late and foggy in meetings.

Anyway, that’s the whirlwind. How are you holding up?

Love,
Michael

metadata.subject: Catch‑up: Dad, Jacob, your case, and my back

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Anderson <m.anderson@northriveranalytics.com>
To: Linda Peterson <l.peterson@example.com>
Date: 2025-12-12
Subject: Quick update on Emily, the kids, and my back

Hi Mom,

I realized I never really told you the full story about how things went when Emily was in the ER at Regions Hospital on December 7–8. It was a long weekend, but in a weird way it showed me how far my back has come since I started making those adjustments in early December 2024.

With Emily at Regions for the panic attack, it was basically a full single-parenting weekend at the house on Brookstone Lane. I was carrying Sophie up and down the stairs for naps and bedtime, helping Jacob in and out of the tub, hauling laundry, all the usual chaos. The surprising part was that my lower back pain stayed around a 3 out of 10 the whole time instead of spiking to an 8 out of 10 like it did during her first episode last spring. I was tired, but I wasn’t wrecked or locked up on the couch afterward.

I’ve been keeping up with the stretches the PT showed me and using the standing desk more consistently. It’s not perfect, but weekends like that make me feel a little more confident that I can handle things when life gets messy.

Emily’s doing better now—she’s following up with her therapist and her primary care doctor, and we’re trying to be more proactive about stress. The kids bounced back pretty quickly; Jacob just wanted to know exactly how the ER works, and Sophie mostly wanted extra bedtime stories.

Love,
Michael

metadata.subject: Quick update on Emily, the kids, and my back

artifacts.content:
Journal – 2025-09-06

I’ve been thinking a lot about how my mood has shifted as the pain eased up this fall. Day to day I’m less snappy with Emily and the kids, but there’s still this pocket of anger and shame that flares up whenever Dad is involved.

Labor Day weekend 2025 was a prime example. Dad flew in from Arizona and stayed with us at the house on Brookstone Lane here in Woodbury. One night after the kids went to bed, he made that “joke” in front of Emily about me “ending up just like Uncle Mark with his pills.” I felt my chest tighten and my jaw clench instantly. It was like I was right back in March 2023, driving Uncle Mark up to Hazelden Betty Ford in Center City, Minnesota, to get him admitted for opioid addiction. I remember filling out the inpatient admission paperwork and thinking, “I cannot let my life go off the rails like this.”

Dad’s comment hit every nerve. I wanted to yell, slam a door, something. Instead, I went into the kitchen, did the 4-7-8 breathing exercise my therapist showed me, and stared out the window until my shoulders dropped. I still felt a ton of resentment, but at least I didn’t take it out on Jacob and Sophie the next day.

There’s progress in being able to see the pattern in real time. Still a lot to unpack around Dad and Mark, but at least I’m not blindly repeating it with my own kids.

Notes to self:

- How Would You Rate Your Progress Toward Those Goals So Far Goal 3 Rating: 3.0
- Your Improvements So Far Is Taking Longer Than Expected: false
- Your Improvements So Far Is Occurring Faster Than Expected: false
